profile.engineering.title: Engineering Background
profile.engineering.summary: GitHub has been my main public engineering record since 2017, covering a much longer arc of building, experiments, and open-source work than my publication list alone.

body.label: Long-Term Record
body.text: If you want the longer arc of what I have built, GitHub is usually the better source than Google Scholar. It has been my main public engineering record since 2017 and shows projects, experiments, and shifts in interest across many years.

body.label: Builder Trajectory
body.text: I got into programming early and kept using GitHub as the place where ideas became code, from small experiments and utilities to larger open-source tools and research-adjacent systems.

body.label: Current Style
body.text: I still think through software by building. My work often sits between developer tools, agent workflows, interfaces, and practical systems that people can actually use.

profile.scholar.title: Research Publication Snapshot
profile.scholar.summary: Google Scholar is the publication-facing view of my work: useful for papers and citations, but much narrower than the GitHub record of what I build.

body.label: Current Position
body.text: I am a 1st year graduate student at NYCU, working across Human-Computer Interaction, AI agents, and software systems.

body.label: Research Orientation
body.text: My current work asks how people should collaborate with increasingly capable agents, especially in software engineering and high-speed knowledge work.

body.label: Boundary
body.text: Scholar is mainly the publication and citation view of my work. It does not capture the broader history of the software, tools, and experiments I have built over time.

rq-agent-collaboration.title: Multi-Human Steering for Agent Systems
rq-agent-collaboration.summary: How should teams coordinate around a shared agent system when planning, analysis, and execution move faster than any single person can fully review?

body.label: Question
body.text: How should teams coordinate around a shared agent system when planning, analysis, and execution all happen faster than any single person can fully review?

body.label: Why It Matters
body.text: One person should not carry the full cost of understanding, auditing, and steering a high-speed agent. I want collaboration models where people guide the system from different areas of expertise instead of chasing it alone.

body.label: Current Direction
body.text: I am studying interaction models where multiple people provide feedback, constraints, and reinforcement through a unified agent interface so teams can stay aligned without forcing everyone to inspect everything.

rq-agent-software-engineering.title: Interfaces for Agent-Driven Software Engineering
rq-agent-software-engineering.summary: What new development environments, representations, and visualizations do we need when agents can read, modify, and generate code faster than humans can track?

body.label: Question
body.text: What new development environments, representations, and visualizations do we need when agents can read, modify, and generate code faster than humans can track?

body.label: Why It Matters
body.text: Traditional code editors assume humans inspect changes directly, while pure CLI workflows compress away too much context. Neither is enough when agent activity becomes dense, parallel, and continuous.

body.label: Current Direction
body.text: I am exploring workflow designs that make agent behavior observable, reviewable, and steerable so humans can keep judgment and control inside highly automated engineering loops.

rq-software-evolution.title: Shortening the Path from User Need to Software Change
rq-software-evolution.summary: How can software evolve much faster in agent-mediated product teams without losing safety, controllability, or human understanding?

body.label: Question
body.text: How can software evolve much faster in agent-mediated product teams without losing safety, controllability, or human understanding?

body.label: Why It Matters
body.text: As agents get better at turning intent into concrete changes, the distance between end users and software updates can shrink dramatically. We need new processes that preserve oversight while letting products adapt faster.

body.label: Current Direction
body.text: I am thinking about frameworks, governance mechanisms, and transition paths that help teams move into agent-driven work while redirecting human effort toward deeper reasoning, creativity, and cross-domain problem solving.

pub-si-2025-open-track.title: The NTNU System at the S&I Challenge 2025 SLA Open Track
pub-si-2025-open-track.summary: A shared-task system paper on spoken language assessment that combines complementary speech and multimodal signals in one evaluation pipeline.

attributes.authors: HY Lin, TH Lo, YH Fang, JK Lin, CC Wang, HC Lu, B Chen

body.label: Contribution
body.text: A shared-task system paper on spoken language assessment that combines complementary speech and multimodal signals in a single evaluation pipeline.

body.label: Summary
body.text: It documents the NTNU approach, task setup, and practical tradeoffs involved in building a competitive challenge submission.

pub-asa-multifaceted-2025.title: Advancing Automated Speaking Assessment Leveraging Multifaceted Relevance and Grammar Information
pub-asa-multifaceted-2025.summary: A study of automated speaking assessment that brings together relevance and grammar signals instead of relying on a single coarse judgment.

attributes.authors: HC Lu, JK Lin, HY Lin, CC Wang, B Chen

body.label: Contribution
body.text: A study of automated speaking assessment that brings together relevance and grammar signals instead of relying on a single coarse judgment.

body.label: Summary
body.text: The experiments examine how richer linguistic structure can improve scoring reliability and make the model's reasoning easier to analyze.

pub-hesitation-tagging-2025.title: Acoustically Precise Hesitation Tagging Is Essential for End-to-End Verbatim Transcription Systems
pub-hesitation-tagging-2025.summary: A transcription study showing that hesitation should be modeled explicitly rather than collapsed away in end-to-end verbatim ASR systems.

attributes.authors: JK Lin, HC Lu, CC Wang, HY Lin, B Chen

body.label: Contribution
body.text: A transcription study showing that hesitation should be modeled explicitly rather than collapsed away in end-to-end verbatim ASR systems.

body.label: Summary
body.text: The results show that preserving disfluency as first-class information improves fidelity for realistic speech transcription tasks.

project-d1-manager.title: d1-manager
project-d1-manager.summary: A Cloudflare D1 workbench for browsing schema, editing rows, running SQL, and turning natural-language questions into executable queries.

body.label: Overview
body.text: I built d1-manager to make Cloudflare D1 easier to inspect and debug in practice, combining schema browsing, direct row editing, SQL execution, and semantic query support in one workflow.

project-smart-whisper.title: smart-whisper
project-smart-whisper.summary: A Node.js addon around whisper.cpp focused on practical model loading, offloading, and runtime integration for speech applications.

body.label: Overview
body.text: smart-whisper wraps whisper.cpp for Node.js so speech applications can load models, offload work, and integrate inference into runtime systems with less friction.

project-leetcode-stats-card.title: LeetCode-Stats-Card
project-leetcode-stats-card.summary: A Cloudflare-hosted card generator that turns LeetCode activity into customizable, embeddable visuals for GitHub profiles and personal sites.

body.label: Overview
body.text: LeetCode-Stats-Card packages LeetCode activity as shareable cards with themes, font options, styling hooks, and optional extensions so people can embed a polished status view without maintaining their own renderer.

project-selflare.title: selflare
project-selflare.summary: A CLI that compiles Cloudflare Workers into Cap'n Proto configs and ships them as minimal Docker deployments while preserving common platform bindings.

body.label: Overview
body.text: I built selflare to run Cloudflare-style applications outside the managed platform, compiling workers into deployable configs and generating Docker setups that keep bindings like KV, D1, R2, durable objects, and Cache API usable locally.

project-gradio-rs.title: gradio-rs
project-gradio-rs.summary: A Rust client and CLI for Gradio apps with API discovery, file uploads, queued jobs, and both blocking and async inference.

body.label: Overview
body.text: gradio-rs brings Gradio interactions into Rust with API inspection, upload handling, blocking and queued predictions, and a CLI so model demos can be driven from code or the terminal.

project-rhythm-rs.title: rhythm-rs
project-rhythm-rs.summary: A Rust rhythm-game workspace that separates engine timing, Taiko rules, chart parsing, and a playable game into reusable crates.

body.label: Overview
body.text: I built rhythm-rs to explore rhythm-game systems in a modular way, splitting core timing and note logic, Taiko-specific behavior, TJA parsing, and the playable game into separate Rust crates.

project-jacoblin-cool-site.title: jacoblin.cool
project-jacoblin-cool-site.summary: The repository for this personal site, combining a SvelteKit frontend, a structured knowledge bundle, and an authenticated chat backend that lets visitors interview me through an agent.

body.label: Overview
body.text: This site is not just a static homepage. It is a SvelteKit application backed by a structured knowledge registry and a chat system that lets visitors ask questions about my work and get grounded answers.

body.label: Architecture
body.text: The repository is a pnpm monorepo with the site app and a shared agent package. The frontend is built with SvelteKit, while the backend chat route streams answers from a verified knowledge bundle and live tool outputs.

body.label: Infrastructure
body.text: The app uses Firebase authentication and Firestore-backed conversation state, and the chat runtime is designed to answer from site content first while still being able to fetch live GitHub details when needed.
